claim.content:
The diagnostic distinction is that a Brinkman–Rice quasi-localized Fermi-liquid scenario retains a sharp Fermi surface whose volume counts electrons according to Luttinger’s theorem even as quasiparticle weight and effective mass change, whereas a Hubbard split-band (charge-transfer) scenario may lack a sharp Fermi surface that counts Cu-derived carriers; angle-resolved or high-quality angle-integrated photoemission spectroscopy, as performed in the cited studies, provides sufficiently direct information about both the existence of a sharp Fermi-surface locus and its enclosed momentum-space volume that, subject to the usual experimental caveats of surface sensitivity, energy and momentum resolution, and matrix-element effects, photoemission is the most practical experimental discriminator between the Brinkman–Rice and split-band pictures.
      [3]
